title: Chapter 3: NullPointer
content: "We should meet. IRL. I don't trust encrypted voice for this."

Chloe's voice crackled through Maya's headphones — the compression artifacts of their Discord call turning every sibilant into static. Maya pulled the headphones off one ear, as if that would make the request more reasonable.

"In person?" Maya said. The words felt foreign. She hadn't left her apartment in two days. The takeout containers on her counter formed a kind of archaeological record — Tuesday's pad thai stratified beneath Thursday's pho. She'd been bathing in Lucy's audio files, drowning in them, and the walls of her studio apartment had become the only geometry that made sense.
"I know a place," Chloe said. "No cameras. Dark enough that even your audio paranoia won't be a problem."

Maya's fingers hovered over the keyboard. Her audio paranoia — as Chloe had taken to calling it — wasn't unfounded. Three days ago she'd discovered that the Lumina app was accessing phone microphones during guided meditation sessions. Not surprising for a tech company, but the *why* of it still gnawed at her.
Chloe read her silence correctly. "447 Second Street. Oakland, Jack London District. Warehouse with a red door, no signage. I'll leave it unlocked. Come alone, or don't come at all."
The line went dead.

Maya wrote the address on the back of a receipt from Pho 84. She stared at it. Then she grabbed her laptop, slotted it into her bag alongside the portable audio interface — the Focusrite Scarlett that had been Lucy's gift to her when they'd moved into their first shared apartment, the one Maya had kept when everything else went into boxes or the trash.

She paused at the door. On the hook where she always hung them, her Sony MDR-7506s waited — the same model every audio engineer owned, black pads worn soft from years of use. Maya took them down and looped them around her neck. The weight settled against her collarbones like a talisman.
Then she opened the door and walked into the world.

The warehouse had no sign. Just a red door between a wholesale seafood distributor and a building wrapped in construction scaffolding, plastic sheeting snapping in the October wind. The Jack London District smelled like brine and diesel and something chemical underneath — the residue of a hundred industries stacked on top of each other, each one leaving its ghost.
Maya pushed the door open and stepped inside.

The cold hit her first — not refrigerated, but the kind of chill that seeps out of concrete and brick when the sun goes down. Then the sound: a low, composite hum that her ears immediately began to separate into components. Server fans, multiple units. The click of a mechanical keyboard. The buzz of fluorescent lights fighting against the blue glow of three oversized monitors mounted on a rig that looked like it had been welded together from plumbing parts.
Then the smell: solder and burnt coffee, layered over Monster Energy and something that might have been instant ramen reconstituted one too many times.
"Shut the door. The dust fucks with my rig."

Chloe Park was sitting in an Aeron chair that probably cost more than the entire rest of the setup combined, her back to the entrance, fingers never stopping their percussion across the keyboard. She didn't turn around.
Maya shut the door. The lock clicked — mechanical, not electronic. She noticed that immediately.
"Sit anywhere. Not the beanbag. The cat situation is unresolved."

Maya looked around. The space was a converted shipping warehouse — exposed brick walls, original hardwood floors scarred by decades of forklifts, and enough cable running across the ceiling to strap down a cargo ship. Server racks lined one wall, their indicator lights blinking in patterns that probably meant something. Three monitors cast blue light across Chloe's workspace, and stacked against the far wall were enough Monster Energy cans to build a small fortress. The Lo-Carb variety, mostly. Some Ultra White. A few original greens at the base like foundation stones.
Chloe finally swiveled around.

She was nothing like what Maya had expected. Twenty-four, Korean-American, with dark hair pulled back in a knot held together by what appeared to be a single chopstick. Fingerless gloves even though the space was heated — the kind with the fold-back mitten caps, currently flapped open. She wore a Bay Area Rapid Transit hoodie, the old blue one with the train logo faded to ghost, and no makeup. Her eyes were bloodshot, the kind of red that spoke of days, not hours. She looked like she hadn't slept since the Obama administration.
But behind her, mounted on a light stand, was a ring light. Professional grade. The kind podcasters used to make their faces look human at 2 AM.
"You podcast?" Maya asked.

"Sometimes. When I need to yell at the cloud and want an archive." Chloe's voice was different in person — flatter, more deliberate, like she was rationing expression. "Sit. I'll show you what I've got."
Chloe's setup was insane.

Maya had worked in recording studios with six-figure signal chains and she'd never seen anything quite like this. A Raspberry Pi cluster hummed in a rack mount beside the server towers — eight units, each running a custom monitoring dashboard that cycled through data streams too fast for Maya to parse. On the central monitor, a terminal window scrolled green text like a waterfall made of coordinates and timestamps.
But it was the wall that stopped her.
Chloe called it her Aeterna war board.

It covered twelve feet of exposed brick — Post-it notes in four colors, connected by lengths of black string and red yarn that traced relationships between entities. Corporate filings printed and pinned. Screenshots of app interfaces. Headshots of executives clipped from LinkedIn. A branching diagram that mapped Aeterna Wellness's corporate structure from the parent company down through eleven subsidiaries, half of which Maya had never heard of. At the center, circled three times in red Sharpie: AETERNA CORE — ACCESS DENIED.
"You've been building this for months," Maya said. It wasn't a question.

"Six. Alone." Chloe pulled her chair up to the war board and crossed her arms, fingerless gloves making the gesture look more tactical than casual. "Every shell company. Every board member. Every patent filing. Every property they own or lease in the continental United States plus three in Switzerland and one in Singapore that they're definitely not supposed to have."
"Why?" Maya looked at her. "Why do you care this much?"
Chloe's jaw shifted. Something flickered behind her eyes — there and gone, like a window closing.
"Because tech bros are a disease and someone has to be the antibiotic."

It was a good line. Practiced, even. The kind of thing you said in a podcast intro. Maya let it sit, and in the silence, she noticed the second monitor — the one Chloe was angled away from. On it, minimized but not closed, was a photo. A younger Chloe, maybe nineteen or twenty, standing on the MIT campus with the Great Dome behind her. Her arm was around someone Maya didn't recognize — another young woman, curly hair, bright smile. The kind of photo you kept open because you looked at it every day.
Chloe followed her gaze. Her hand moved fast — Alt-W, minimize — and the photo vanished into the taskbar.
"Focus," Chloe said. "I'll show you the Lumina app."

They pulled up side by side, Chloe's chair and a folding metal one she'd positioned for Maya. The server hum formed a low cushion of white noise beneath their conversation, punctuated by keyboard clicks and the occasional ping of a monitoring script flagging something.

Chloe opened a terminal and started typing. "I've been inside their API for three weeks. Read-only, they don't know I'm there. I mapped the data architecture first, but the real treasure is in the user profiles." She pulled up a dashboard — anonymized data, rows upon rows of health metrics, biometric readouts, genetic markers. "They're collecting everything. Heart rate variability, sleep cycles, blood oxygen, cortisol levels from the phone's microphone via stress detection in vocal patterns. But here's where it gets interesting."

She typed a query. Filters stacked: blood type, AB negative. Mitochondrial haplogroup H1a. Age 18–30. Health profile indicating, in Lumina's clinical language, "optimal cellular regeneration potential."
The dashboard returned 847 results.
Eight hundred and forty-seven women. Cataloged like commodities — sortable, filterable, ranked.
Maya's hands were cold. The warehouse air had nothing to do with it.
"Lucy's on this list," she said.
"Scroll." Chloe's voice was even. Clinical. "She's number twelve."

Maya scrolled. Row twelve. An anonymized ID hash — but she didn't need a name. She recognized the age. The blood type. The haplogroup that Lucy had once told her about over ramen, laughing about how rare it was, how she was basically a unicorn.
"By their scoring algorithm," Chloe said, "she's a 'Platinum Specimen.'"

The word landed like a slap. Specimen. Lucy — who sang off-key in the shower and left orange peels on every surface and once cried during a nature documentary about fungi — reduced to a ranking on a corporate spreadsheet.
Maya's jaw tightened. She didn't trust herself to speak.
"Hey." Chloe's voice dropped a register. Not soft — Chloe didn't do soft — but aimed. Focused. "We use what they gave us. They made the list. We read it. That's how we find the door."
Maya breathed. Nodded. Then she reached into her bag.

"There's something else." She pulled out the Focusrite and connected it to Chloe's auxiliary input. "At the end of the last recording — Lucy's last recording — there's a sound. Buried beneath the breathing. I almost missed it."
She queued the file. The 0.7 Hz breathing filled the space — slow, mechanical, wrong. And then, at the tail end, the clicking. Rhythmic. Deliberate. Patterns too structured to be artifact.
Chloe's eyes widened.

She grabbed a pen from the desk — a felt-tip, the kind you'd use for circuit board markup — and started writing on the back of a Monster Energy receipt. Dots and dashes. Her hand moved in bursts, keeping time with the audio, translating in real-time.
A — E — T — E — R — N — A
Space.
S — U — B
Space.
B
Space.
A — L — I — V — E
Chloe set the pen down. Her hand was trembling.
"Aeterna Sublevel B," she read aloud. "Alive."
Neither of them spoke. The server hum filled the silence. Somewhere in the warehouse, a pipe ticked — thermal expansion, the building breathing.
"She's alive," Maya said. The words left her mouth and she realized she hadn't fully believed it until now. Hoped, yes. Believed? No. "She's alive and she's sending messages."

"Through her recording equipment, or through something near her that's acting as a transmitter," Chloe said, her mind already running ahead. "The audio interface could have been modified. Or there's something in the room with her that picks up mechanical input — tapping, clicking — and modulates it into the signal. Either way." She looked up at Maya, and for the first time there was something behind the tactical flatline of her expression. Something like awe. "If she's sending Morse code, she's conscious. She's strategic. And she's running out of time before whoever is keeping her figures it out."

Maya's hand went to her neck, fingers finding the cold metal of the headphones. The weight of them. Lucy had given her these. Lucy had taught her to listen. And Lucy was in a basement somewhere, tapping out messages in code, waiting for someone to hear.
Someone had.
Chloe closed her laptop.

Maya blinked. In the time they'd been working, the blue monitor glow had become the only light in the room — the overhead fluorescents had been off for who knows how long. Chloe's face was half-lit, half-shadow, and in that divided light she looked at Maya directly for the first time.
Not at her screens. Not at the war board. At Maya.

"I've been working alone because I don't trust people," Chloe said. "But I can't crack Aeterna's internal network from the outside. Their security is military-grade. Not corporate military — *military* military. AES-256 encryption on everything, hardware security modules I've never seen outside of DARPA procurement logs, and a traffic routing pattern that looks like nothing I've encountered in the private sector. Something beyond corporate. Something that doesn't make sense for a wellness company."
She took a breath. The fingerless gloves flexed.
"I need someone on the inside." A pause. "And you just happened to mention you're an audio engineer."
Maya saw it coming. Felt it arrive like a train she'd been standing on the tracks for.

She should say no. She should go home, lock her door, put on her headphones, and drown in Lucy's recordings until the guilt ate through her completely. That was the trajectory she'd been on. The comfortable arc of self-destruction.

But Lucy had tapped out *A-L-I-V-E* in Morse code while kept prisoner in a basement. Lucy had believed someone would hear. Lucy — strategic, conscious, running out of time — had sent a message into the dark.
And Maya had heard it.
"Tell me what you need me to do."
Chloe smiled.

It was the first real expression Maya had seen on her face — not a podcast intro smile, not a deflection. This was sharp and dark and maybe a little unhinged, the smile of someone who'd been working alone for six months and finally had someone to work with. It transformed her. Made her look less like a sleep-deprived shut-in and more like what she probably was underneath: dangerous.
"Oh, you're going to love this."
Chloe reached across the desk and pulled the laptop back open. Her fingers moved across the keyboard, and a new window filled the center monitor.
A floor plan.

Complex, multi-level, with subterranean sections marked in hatching that indicated reinforced construction. Labels in a technical font: SUBLEVEL A — STORAGE. SUBLEVEL B — AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY. And at the bottom, in a section Chloe had highlighted in neon green: AUDIO ENGINEERING BAY — CONTRACTOR ACCESS.
Maya leaned in. Her headphones pressed cold against her collarbone.
"That," Chloe said, tapping the screen, "is where you're going."
The server hummed. The blue light held them.
Somewhere beneath the floor plan, in a basement Maya couldn't see, Lucy Chen was waiting.
And they were coming.
